assumptions: Traffic: very low today (<1k concurrent dynamic requests); all three tiers share this load — tiers differ in robustness, not traffic level.
Single AWS region (us-east-1 or us-west-2); no multi-region requirement.
No regulated data (no PCI/HIPAA); standard security floor applies.

PostGIS extension required — RDS for PostgreSQL is the only managed option that supports it; Aurora Serverless v2 supports PostGIS and is used for budget tier as the cheapest managed Postgres-compatible option.
Headless Chromium render service uses Lambda with a Chromium layer (e.g., @sparticuz/chromium); rendered output >6 MB written to S3 and returned by reference.
ISR revalidation is on-demand (Next.js revalidateTag/revalidatePath) — the Node container holds the ISR in-memory/disk cache; CloudFront is the CDN layer.
Background AI pipeline orchestrator uses pg-boss or similar Postgres-backed job queue — it is a lightweight coordinator, not a heavy compute node.
Nightly backup via RDS automated snapshots with 14-day retention; data-reconciliation cron via EventBridge Scheduler invoking Lambda.
Observability: CloudWatch Logs + alarms + SNS email for budget; dashboards + X-Ray tracing added at balanced; PagerDuty escalation at resilient.
No ElastiCache in budget tier (traffic too low to justify); introduced at balanced for read offload.

Budget tier uses single public subnet (no NAT) for the container — but the data tier (RDS) goes in a private subnet per the security floor for VPC-bound stores. One NAT gateway is therefore unavoidable in all tiers where RDS is present.
Fargate is chosen over EC2 for the web/orchestrator containers — no instance management, right-sized per service.
Lambda render function timeout set to 5 min (max), with reserved concurrency to cap downstream RDS/S3 burst.
Cost estimates are on-demand list prices for us-east-1, presented as ranges over assumed monthly volumes. They exclude the AWS Free Tier, Savings Plans, Reserved Instances, and negotiated discounts.
Assumed traffic ~100k–1M requests/month, applied equally to all three tiers (tiers differ by robustness, not traffic). Set "expected monthly visitors" in intake to change it.
clarificationsUsed: Cost-optimized: single-AZ acceptable for budget tier.
Very low traffic today; growth in cached content + pipeline throughput, not concurrent dynamic load.
PostGIS is load-bearing — must use a managed Postgres that supports it.
Render output can exceed 6 MB — must write to S3 and return by reference.
Background pipeline must be decoupled from the web request path.
Headless Chromium is spiky/idle — scale-to-zero Lambda is the right fit.

tiers.name: budget
tiers.summary: Single-AZ, cost-optimized. One Fargate task each for the Next.js web tier and the orchestrator. RDS PostgreSQL (db.t4g.micro) in a private subnet. Lambda for the render service (scale-to-zero). CloudFront + WAF at the edge. SQS decouples the web tier from the pipeline. One NAT gateway (unavoidable — private RDS). No ElastiCache (traffic too low).

edges.payload: rendered HTML/screenshot (>6 MB via S3 PUT)
metric filters (error rate, latency, DLQ depth)

delta: Baseline: single-AZ, single Fargate task per service, db.t4g.micro RDS w/ PostGIS.
CloudFront + WAF at edge; in-process ISR cache on the single web task.
Lambda render service: scale-to-zero, reserved concurrency=5, S3 output for >6 MB payloads.
SQS + DLQ decouples web → orchestrator pipeline; orch polls at its own rate.
One NAT gateway (unavoidable: private-subnet RDS requires it for app-tier egress).
Observability: CloudWatch Logs (30d), golden-signal alarms → SNS → email.
No ElastiCache, no read replicas, no multi-AZ: cost floor, not hardened.
tradeoffs: Single-AZ: RDS or Fargate AZ loss = downtime (RPO/RTO relaxed, acceptable at current scale).
No ElastiCache: hot reads hit RDS directly; fine at low traffic, bottleneck at scale.
Single NAT gateway: AZ failure loses all private-subnet egress.
In-process ISR cache: lost on Fargate task recycle; CloudFront masks most of it.
db.t4g.micro: minimal compute; vertical resize needed before high pipeline throughput.

costDrivers.note: Data transfer out to internet, first 10TB/month tier (North America). First 1TB/month is free.
HTTPS requests ($0.0075 per 10,000). North America/Europe.
Per web ACL/month. Add ~$1.00 per rule/month and ~$0.60 per 1M requests inspected.
Application Load Balancer per-hour charge (always-on).
Assumed-throughput estimate: per Load Balancer Capacity Unit-hour; LCUs scale with connections, requests, and bandwidth.
Assumed-throughput estimate: per GB memory-hour (Linux/x86). Total = memoryGB x hours.
Assumed-throughput estimate: per vCPU-hour (Linux/x86). Total = vCPU x hours.
Compute duration: $0.0000166667 per GB-second (x86). Multiply by memoryGB x durationSeconds per invocation.
On-demand list price ($0.20 per 1M requests). Excludes Free Tier.
Standard queue requests ($0.40 per 1M). First 1M/month free. FIFO is $0.50 per 1M.
S3 Standard storage, first 50TB/month tier.
PUT/COPY/POST/LIST requests per 1,000. GET/SELECT requests are ~$0.0004 per 1,000.
Assumed-throughput estimate: db.t4g.micro on-demand (us-east-1). Cost = instance count × running hours; storage/IO billed separately, multi-AZ via the tier multiplier.
Per GB processed. Forced by the private-subnet data-tier default (security baseline no-public-data-tier); the most common budget surprise.
Per NAT gateway-hour (always-on, ~$32.40/month per AZ before data processing).
Custom event-bus events: $1.00 per 1M events published. Pipes/schema registry priced separately.
CloudWatch Logs ingestion $0.50/GB (first 10GB/mo free in some accounts). Archival storage is cheaper; Insights queries extra.
Standard topic API requests ($0.50 per 1M). Delivery (HTTP/SMS/email) priced separately.
Required by the private-subnet security default (no public data tier) — the most common budget surprise. Inter-AZ data transfer, charged $0.01/GB in each direction.
Required by the private-subnet security default (no public data tier) — the most common budget surprise. Data transfer out to internet, first 10TB/month tier. First 100GB/month is free.

tiers.name: balanced
tiers.summary: Multi-AZ within one region. ALB spans two AZs, Fargate services scale horizontally, RDS upgrades to db.t4g.small Multi-AZ, ElastiCache (cache.t4g.micro) added for read offload and ISR shared cache, two NAT gateways (one per AZ). Lambda render service unchanged.

edges.payload: rendered HTML/screenshot (>6 MB via S3 PUT)
metric filters (error rate, latency, DLQ depth)

delta: RDS → Multi-AZ standby (db.t4g.small): automatic failover ~60s, no data loss.
ElastiCache Redis (cache.t4g.micro) added: shared ISR cache across web tasks + hot-read offload.
Fargate web + orch services: min 2 tasks across 2 AZs; ALB health-check-driven failover.
Two NAT gateways (one per AZ): eliminates single-AZ NAT as an egress SPOF.
X-Ray tracing added across web → orch → render path.
CloudWatch dashboard added for golden-signal visibility.
tradeoffs: Two NAT gateways and Multi-AZ RDS add significant always-on cost vs budget.
ElastiCache is a new always-on node (~$15/mo); overkill if concurrent web tasks stay at 1.
RDS Multi-AZ standby is passive (no reads); read replicas needed for read scale-out.
Still single-region: a region outage takes the whole stack down.

tiers.name: resilient
tiers.summary: Cross-region DR + production-grade hardening. RDS promoted to Aurora PostgreSQL (Multi-AZ cluster + cross-region read replica for DR). Fargate services auto-scale with target-tracking. ElastiCache upgraded to a Multi-AZ cluster. Render Lambda gets provisioned concurrency for warm-start on city onboarding bursts. EventBridge replaces direct SQS polling for fan-out. PagerDuty escalation on critical alarms.

nodes.role: Aurora Multi-AZ cluster w/ PostGIS (db.r8g.large writer)

edges.payload: rendered HTML/screenshot (>6 MB via S3 PUT)
metric filters (error rate, latency, DLQ depth)

delta: RDS → Aurora PostgreSQL Multi-AZ cluster: writer + 2 readers, cross-region replica for DR.
ElastiCache → Multi-AZ Redis cluster with auto-failover.
Lambda render: provisioned concurrency=2 eliminates cold-start on city onboarding bursts.
EventBridge bus replaces direct SQS publish from web: decoupled fan-out, rule-based routing.
Shield Advanced added for DDoS cost protection and DRT access.
PagerDuty escalation topic wired to critical CloudWatch alarms (P1/P2 SLO breaches).
Fargate services: target-tracking auto-scaling (CPU 60%, ALB req/task) + min 2 tasks.
SLO alarms: p99 latency and error-rate budget burn alerts on CloudWatch.
tradeoffs: Aurora + cross-region replica and Shield Advanced add substantial cost — justified only at SLA-bearing scale.
Provisioned Lambda concurrency is a fixed charge even when idle; right-size to city-onboarding burst pattern.
EventBridge adds routing flexibility but another hop to reason about in incident triage.
Cross-region replica is read-only DR, not active-active; promotion requires manual intervention or Global Database upgrade.

keyDecisions.decision: Compute model for the Next.js web tier
keyDecisions.chosen: Fargate (single task, single AZ) behind ALB
keyDecisions.rationale: Persistent Node server with ISR in-process cache and on-demand revalidation requires a long-running container runtime; Lambda's stateless cold-start model breaks ISR consistency, and App Runner lacks the routing control needed for the /admin split — Fargate on a t-class CPU reservation is the cheapest always-on container option (Performance Efficiency + Cost Optimization).

keyDecisions.decision: Compute model for the orchestrator (job queue) tier
keyDecisions.rationale: pg-boss requires a persistent Postgres connection for reliable job polling; Lambda's stateless model wastes connection slots and adds reconnect latency — a small always-on Fargate task decoupled from the web service scales independently and is connection-pool-friendly (Reliability + Cost Optimization).

keyDecisions.decision: Compute model for the headless Chromium render service
keyDecisions.chosen: Lambda with Chromium layer (reserved concurrency, S3 output for large payloads)
keyDecisions.rationale: Spiky, idle-most-of-the-time workload with hard pay-per-invocation requirement — Lambda's scale-to-zero is the only model that costs $0 when idle; Fargate scale-to-zero has a ~2-min cold start making synchronous orchestrator calls impractical (Cost Optimization + Performance Efficiency).

keyDecisions.decision: Primary datastore: managed Postgres with PostGIS
keyDecisions.chosen: RDS for PostgreSQL (db.t4g.micro single-AZ) at budget; Aurora PostgreSQL at balanced/resilient
alternativesConsidered: Aurora Serverless v2 for all tiers
keyDecisions.rationale: PostGIS is load-bearing; RDS for PostgreSQL supports it at the lowest always-on price point (db.t4g.micro ~$13/mo) which beats Aurora Serverless v2's minimum ACU cost at near-zero traffic — Aurora's serverless scaling wins only when traffic variance is high, which balanced+ justifies (Cost Optimization → Reliability step-up).

keyDecisions.chosen: Single NAT gateway in all tiers (required by private-subnet RDS)
keyDecisions.rationale: RDS in a private subnet requires a NAT gateway for the Fargate tasks in the app subnet to reach it — this is the unavoidable always-on cost of the private-data-tier baseline; there is no serverless-no-VPC shape when PostGIS on RDS is load-bearing (Security + Cost transparency).

keyDecisions.chosen: CloudFront in front of ALB; Next.js in-process ISR cache on the Fargate task
keyDecisions.rationale: At very low traffic, CloudFront absorbs the read burst and the single Fargate task's in-process ISR cache is sufficient; ElastiCache adds ~$15–25/mo always-on cost with no benefit until multiple web tasks need a shared cache (Cost Optimization → introduced at balanced).

keyDecisions.decision: Render output delivery for payloads >6 MB
keyDecisions.chosen: Lambda writes rendered HTML/screenshot to S3; returns a pre-signed URL to the orchestrator
keyDecisions.rationale: Lambda's 6 MB synchronous response limit is a hard constraint; S3 pre-signed URLs are the canonical pattern — atomic, durable, and CDN-cacheable without EFS's added latency and VPC complexity (Reliability + Performance Efficiency).

keyDecisions.chosen: SQS queue between web tier and orchestrator, with DLQ
alternativesConsidered: Direct Postgres insert from web tier
keyDecisions.rationale: SQS decouples the web request path from pipeline execution, absorbs bursts, and gives at-least-once delivery with a DLQ for poison jobs — the orchestrator pulls at its own rate without coupling to web traffic (Reliability + Operational Excellence).

securityFloor: Encrypt all data at rest (KMS / service-native SSE), preferring customer-managed keys.
Enforce TLS in transit everywhere; terminate HTTPS at the edge and reject plaintext.
Least-privilege IAM: scoped per-service roles, no wildcards, no long-lived access keys.
Enable S3 Block Public Access account-wide so no bucket or object can be made public.
Keep the data tier private: VPC-bound stores in private subnets with no public route; managed/serverless stores (DynamoDB/S3) are already private via IAM and need no VPC or NAT.
Store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store — never in code or images.
Front public endpoints with CloudFront + AWS WAF (managed + rate-based rules); Shield Standard for L3/L4.
Enable CloudTrail plus S3/CloudFront/ALB access logs and VPC Flow Logs for audit.
